summary: DHH explains why he quit Facebook in 2011, arguing that its mission to connect everyone is fundamentally toxic. He rejects Zuckerberg's idea that people should have one consistent identity, arguing that personal growth requires the freedom to grow apart from past social circles. Facebook disrupted the natural process of losing touch by making permanent connection the default, requiring active effort to sever ties. He found that the platform's persistent audience from every life stage inhibited authentic self-expression and experimentation, so he deleted it entirely and found it liberating.
key_points: Zuckerberg's philosophy that people should have one authentic self across all contexts is toxic and stifles personal evolution
Growing apart and losing touch with people is a natural, healthy process that enables personal growth and reinvention
Facebook reversed the default: staying connected became automatic while disconnecting required deliberate, socially awkward action
The persistent audience of past acquaintances moderates what people share and inhibits experimentation with new identities
Real-life transitions like leaving university naturally sever connections, and this is accepted as normal — Facebook broke that
money_quotes: I quit Facebook back in 2011 for a lot of reasons, but perhaps the most crucial was to rebel against its core mission: Connecting the world.
Zuckerberg has repeatedly doubled down on the toxic idea that we should only have one self, one persona. That we should be the same person in all social circles, lest we be "frauds".
I'm happy to be a fraud under that definition.
What allowed me to change and prosper was the freedom to grow apart and lose touch with people.
There's a gravitational force that pulls you into repeating the same circular pattern over and over again. Breaking out of that takes tremendous force.
Facebook changed that. It stunted this natural, gentle process of growing apart and losing touch.

Becoming someone else entails experimenting and failing with new styles and ideas. Not a lot of people are so keen to premiere such vulnerable stages of their evolution in front of an audience that expects them to be that same person they always were forever.
Connected in the sense that an anchor is to a boat. A literal drag.
key_insight: Personal growth depends on the freedom to shed old identities and social connections, a natural process that Facebook's permanent connectivity fundamentally disrupts.
